page_content:
THE TELANGANA PREVENTIVE DETENTION ACT, 1970

An Act to provide for preventive detention like in the State and matters
connected therewith.

Be it enacted by the Legislature of the State of Andhra Pradesh in the
Twenty First Year of the Republic of India as follows : —

1. Short title, extent and commencement:—
(1) This Act may be called the Andhra Pradesh Preventive Detention ,1970.
(2) It extends to the whole of the State of Andhra Pradesh.
(3) It shall be deemed to have come into force on the 1st January, 1970.
2. Definitions: — in this Act, unless the context otherwise requires:—
(a) “Detention order” means an order made under Section 3;
(b) “Government” means the State Government.
3. Power to make orders detaining certain persons: —

(1) The Government may, prevent any person from acting in any manner
prejudicial to:—

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(1) The Government may, prevent any person from acting in any manner
prejudicial to:—

i) The security of the State or the maintenance of public order; or (ii) the
maintenance of supplies and services essential to the community, make an order
for detention.

2) Any of the following officers, namely: —
(a) District Magistrates; (b) Additional District Magistrates specially empowered.

c) The Commissioner of City Police, Hyderabad; may, if satisfied as provided
in sub-section (1), can exercise the power conferred.

(3) An officer shall forthwith report the fact to the Government with the
grounds on which the order has been made and other particulars as in
his opinion have a bearing on the matter and no such order shall remain in
force for more than twelve days in the meantime it has been approved by the
Government.

4. Execution of detention orders: — as that execution of warrants of arrest
under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
4. Execution of detention orders: — as that execution of warrants of arrest
under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.

5. Power to regulate place and conditions of detention: — every person in
respect of whom a detention order has been made shall be liable: —

a. To be detained in such place and under such conditions, including conditions
as to maintenance, discipline and punishment for breaches of discipline, as the
Government may, by general or special order, specify; and

220 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
b. To be removed from one place of detention to another place of detention
in the State.

6. Detention orders not to be invalid or inoperative on certain grounds: —
No detention order shall be invalid or inoperative merely by reason:—

(a) grounds on which the order is made is or are Vague or irrelevant.

(b) That the person to be detained there under is outside the limits
of the territorial jurisdiction of the officer making the order; or

(c) That the place of detention of such person is outside the said limits.
7. Powers in relation to absconding persons:—

(1) If the Government or any officer specified in sub-section (2) of Section 3, as
the case may be, has reason to believe that a person in respect of whom a
detention order has been made has absconded or is concealing himself so
that the order cannot be executed, the Government or officer may:—

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(a) Make a report in writing of the fact to a Magistrate of the first class having
jurisdiction be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may
extend to one year or with fine or with both.

8. Grounds of order of detention to be disclosed to persons affected by
the order:—

1. A person is detained in pursuance of a detention order, but not later than
five days from the date of detention, communicate to him the grounds
earliest opportunity against the order to the Government.

2. Shall require the authority to disclose facts which it considers to be against
the public interest to disclose.

9. Constitution of Advisory Board: —
(1) The Government shall, constitute Advisory Boards.

(2) Every Board shall consist of three persons who are qualified to be
appointed as, Judges of a High Court, such persons shall be appointed by
the Government.

(3) The Government shall appoint one of the members of Board as Chairman.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(3) The Government shall appoint one of the members of Board as Chairman.

10. Maximum period of detention: — Shall be twelve months from the date of
detention.

11. Temporary release of persons detained: —

(1) The Government may at any time direct that any person detained in
pursuance of a detention order may be released for any specified period
either without conditions or upon such conditions specified in the direction
as that person accepts and may at any time cancel his release.

221 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(2) In directing the release of person Government may require him to enter into
a bond with or without sureties.

(3) Such person released shall surrender himself at the time and place, and to
the authority. (4) If such person fails without sufficient cause to surrender
he shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term, which may extend to
two years or with fine or with both.

(4) If such person fails to fulfill the conditions imposed or bond entered by

him, and then shall be liable to pay the penalty thereof.

page_content:
THE TELANGANA HABITUAL OFFENDERS ACT, 1962

An Act to provide for the treatment and training of habitual
offenders

Chapter - I

Preliminary

1. Short title, extent and commencement.—
(a) This Act may be called the Andhra Pradesh Habitual Offenders Act, 1962.
(b) It extends to the whole of the State of Andhra Pradesh.

2. Definitions.- In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires:-

(a) “Corrective settlement” means any place established, approved or certified
as a corrective settlement under Section 13;

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(a) “Corrective settlement” means any place established, approved or certified
as a corrective settlement under Section 13;

(b) “habitual offender” means a person who, during any continuous period of
five years, whether before or after the commencement of this Act or partly
before and partly after such commencement, has been sentenced on
conviction on not less than three occasions after he attained the age of
eighteen years to a substantive term of imprisonment for any one or more of
the scheduled offences committed on different occasions and not so
connected together as to form parts of the same transaction, such sentence
not having been reversed in appeal or on revision:

Chapter - II
Registration of Habitual Offenders and Restriction of their Movements

3. Power of the District Collector to register habitual offenders.—
the District Collector or any officer authorized by him shall, by notice in the
prescribed form to be served, call upon every habitual offender in the district: —

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(a) To appear before him at a time and place therein specified;

b) To enter the name and other prescribed particulars of the habitual offender in
the register; and

(c) To allow his finger and palm impressions, foot prints and photograph to
be taken:

4. Custody of register and alterations therein. —

(a) The register shall be placed in the custody of the Superintendent of Police
who shall, report to the District Collector any alterations to be made
therein.

(b) After the register is placed in the custody of the Superintendent of Police, no
fresh alteration shall be made in the register except under an order in

223 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
writing of the District Collector.

5. Power to take finger and palm impressions, foot prints and
photographs at any time .— The District Collector or any officer authorized
by him in this behalf may at any time order the finger and palm impressions,
foot prints and photographs of any registered offender to be taken.

6. Action to be taken when registered offender changes his ordinary
residence from one district to another within or outside the State. —
Within the State.

7. Duration of registration and re-registration of habitual offenders. —

cancellation, or expiry of duration of registration, a habitual offender
may be re-registered in accordance with the provisions of this Act relating to
registration as often as he is convicted of one or more of the scheduled offences at
any time.
8. Right to make representations against registration, preregistration
etc.— make a representation to the Government .

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
9. Power to restrict movements of required offenders — shall be restricted
in his movements for period not exceeding three years.

Before making any such order, Government shall take into consideration
the following matters, namely: —

(a) The nature of the offences of which the registered offender has been
convicted and the circumstances in which the offences were committed;

(b) Whether the registered offender follows any lawful occupation and whether
such occupation is conducive to honest and settled way of life;

(c) The suitability of the area to which his movements are to be
restricted; and

(d) The manner in which the registered offender may earn his living within
such area, and the adequacy of arrangements which are, or likely to be,
availed there for.

A copy of the order shall be served on the registered offender in the
prescribed manner.

Chapter - III
Corrective Training of Habitual Offenders
10. Power to direct habitual offenders to receive corrective training.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Chapter - III
Corrective Training of Habitual Offenders
10. Power to direct habitual offenders to receive corrective training.

(1) If the government are satisfied from the report of the District Collector by
order in writing, direct that the registered offender shall receive training of a
corrective character for such period not exceeding the duration of his
registration or re- registration as may be specified in the order.

224 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(2) Where a habitual offender who is not more than forty years of age: —

(a) He shall receive corrective training for such term of not less than two
and not exceeding five years as the court may determine.

(3) Before giving any direction under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2), the

Government or the court, as the case may be, shall :-

(a) Take into consideration the physical and mental condition of the
offender and his suitability for receiving corrective training in a
corrective settlement;

(b) Give a reasonable opportunity to the offender to show because why
such direction shall not be given.

Chapter-IV

Penalties and Procedure

11. Penalty for failure to comply with certain provisions of the Act. — A
habitual offender who, without lawful excuse, the burden of proving which shall
lie upon him: —

Section 4; or Section 6; or Section 7 or Section 11, may be arrested

without warrant and shall be punishable,—

(i)

(ii)

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Section 4; or Section 6; or Section 7 or Section 11, may be arrested

without warrant and shall be punishable,—

(i)

(ii)

On first conviction with imprisonment for a term which may extend to six
months or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees or with both;
and

On a second or subsequent conviction, with imprisonment for a_ term,
which may extend to one year or with fine, which may extend to five hundred
rupees or with both?

THE TELANGANA HABITUAL OFFENDERS RULES, 1965
Rules
1. In these rules, unless the context otherwise requires,—
> “Form” means a form annexed to these rules;
> “Registering Authority” means the District Collector;

> “Settler? means a _ registered offender who is placed in any
corrective settlement, established, approved, or certified as a
corrective settlement under Section 13.

2. The Superintendent of Police, shall, from time to time, examine
the cases of individuals residing in the area within his jurisdiction, including
those in jail who come under the definition of “habitual offender” and
address

the Registering Authority in Form I, for registration of habitual offenders.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
the Registering Authority in Form I, for registration of habitual offenders.

3. Every habitual offender in respect of whom it is proposed to make
an entry in the register prescribed under Section 3 of the Act shall be issued
a notice in Form II directing him to show because why such an entry should
not be made. In case it is decided to register the individual, he shall be
issued another notice in Form III directing him to appear before the
Registering Authority.

4. (1) (b) Where personal service of the notice is not possible, the notice
shall be published: —

(i) By insertion in the local District Gazette;
(ii) By pasting a copy of such notice: —
(a) In a conspicuous place in the house.

(b) In every police station within the local limits of which any such
village or town is situate; and

(iii) By the serving a copy of such notice on the Headman of such village.

(c) In the case of those in jail, it shall be served through the Superintendent
of the Jail.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(c) In the case of those in jail, it shall be served through the Superintendent
of the Jail.

(2) After receiving notice he shall be given not less than fifteen days time
either (a) to show cause against the action proposed or (b) to appear in
person for registration as required in Rule 5.

5. (1) The register of habitual offenders in Form V shall be in a loose-leaf
book with a separate sheet for each registered offender. The
acknowledgment of the Finger Print Bureau in Form-X.

(2) The registered offenders of a district shall be assigned serial

226 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
numbers with the abbreviation of the name of the district concerned
(as given in the Appendix) to denote the district of registration.

(3) A removal slip in Form VII shall be inserted in the register of habitual

offenders of the original district with the acknowledgment of the
Superintendent of Police of the district to which he has changed his
residence, for the receipt of the personal sheet.

6. (1) Immediately after the name of a person in the Andhra Pradesh Gazette

as a registered offender, the Superintendent of Police shall take steps to
prepare a personal sheet for him, in duplicate, in Form VIII and record
his fingerprints in Form IX.

7. (1) After the completion of Forms V, VIII and IX, the personal sheet (Form

8.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
7. (1) After the completion of Forms V, VIII and IX, the personal sheet (Form

8.

III) shall be maintained in the District Police Office (The District Crime
Bureau) and the duplicate in the Police Station within the jurisdiction of
which the registered offender resides. The fingerprints in Form IX shall
be sent to the Finger Print Bureau, which shall acknowledge its receipts
in Form X.

(2) All District Collectors shall submit to the Government before the 1st day

of April every year a return in Form XIX, containing a list of persons in
respect of whom action has been taken by them under Section 4 of the
Act during the previous calendar year with the other particulars
referred to in that Form.

(1) Any registered offender who intends to leave permanently or
temporarily, the village in which he is residing shall before his departure,
intimate to the Headman of such village, —

(a) The date on which he intends to start;

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(a) The date on which he intends to start;

(b) The village and the house into which he is changing his residence or
to which he is going

(c) The route by which he intends to proceed;

(d) The time he will take in going to his destination; and

(e) In case he is leaving temporarily, the village in which he is residing,
he probable period of his absence there from, and the route and the
time he will take for the return journey and shall obtain from the
Headman a certificate in Form XIII.

ca

If a registered offender is old, sick or infirm or is otherwise
incapacitated from committing crime and if he has continued to
be of satisfactory conduct for a period of three years after his last
release from jail, the District Collector may exempt a registered offender
from notifying change of residence and to report himself as ordered
under Section 7 or to cancel his registration made under Section 4,

page_content:
10.

on a report from the Superintendent of Police and such exemption or
cancellation shall take effect from the date of its publication in the
Andhra Pradesh Gazette.

If the Superintendent of Police considers that the movements of a

is satisfied that the movements of the registered offender should be

TELANGANA EXCISE ACT, 1968

Telangana Excise Act 1968 is the Law relating to the production,
manufacture, possession, transport, purchase and sale of intoxicating
liquors, the levy of duties of excise and countervailing duties on alcoholic
liquors for human consumption and preventive steps by enforcing penal
provision of Excise Act, investigation, sanction of establishment of Excise
Dept. power of Officers, issuing of license etc.

The Prohibition & Excise department contributes the second highest
revenue to the State Exchequer.

The Police Officers as well as Excise Dept. Officers should curb the
offences under the relevant provisions of the Act by making all efforts

(a) To put down possession and sale of non-duty paid Indian Liquor &
beer, illicit distillation and adulteration,

(b) control illicit distillation of arrack and its sale,

(c) put down adulteration of toddy by licensees of toddy shop,
(d) regulate the working of I.L. and F.L. shops and

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(c) put down adulteration of toddy by licensees of toddy shop,
(d) regulate the working of I.L. and F.L. shops and

(e) Control the illicit tapping of trees by frequent checks.

In this Act Sec. 34 to 50-A contain penalties and Secs. 51-64 contains
investigation, detection and trial procedures.

Sec.2 of the Act explains terms and words used in the Act.

Sec.2 (9) Excisable Article:- It means any alcoholic liquor for human
consumption or any intoxicating drug.

Ordinary meaning of Excise:- A Govt. tax on certain goods manufactured,
sold or used within a country.

The Excise is imposed on beer, spirit or on any intoxicant drinks or
substances.

Sec. 2(19):- "Intoxicant" means any liquor as defined in clause (21) or any
intoxicating drug as defined in clause (20) and includes gulmohwa

Sec.2(21): - "Liquor" includes
(a) Spirits of wine, denatured spirits, methylated spirits, rectified spirit,
wine, beer, toddy and every liquid consisting of or containing alcohol; an

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(b) Any other intoxicating substance declared to be liquor by notification of
the Govt.

Sec.2(30): Toddy means juice drawn from an excise tree and containing
alcohol; If it is unfermented it is called 'Neera". After sunrise fermentation
starts and it becomes alcoholic juice called as toddy.

page_content:
Arrack: It means country liquor including arrack brewed, coloured,
flavoured or — spiced.

Sec.13 of the Act:- Manufacture, cultivation of hemp plant, taping of excise
tree or drawing toddy from any such tree, construction or working of
distillery or brewery, sale of liquor using or keeping any materials and
utensils for manufacturing liquors are prohibited except under licence.

Sec.14:- Govt. by issuing a notification prescribed maximum quantities
which can a person possess without license. Possessing more quantities
than the prescribed require license.

G.O. MS.No. 268 Revenue (Ex III) 1st April, 1997

Maximum Quantity of Intoxicants which a person may have in possession
without permit.

page_content:
G.O. MS.No. 268 Revenue (Ex III) 1st April, 1997

Maximum Quantity of Intoxicants which a person may have in possession
without permit.

1. Indian Liquor : Six quart bottles each 750 m.1.
2. Foreign Liquor : Six quart bottles each 750 m.1.
3. Denatured Spirit : Three bulk liters.
4. Methylated Spirit : Three bulk liters.
5. Beer : Twelve bottles of each 650 m.1.
6. Toddy : Two bulk liters
7. Rectified Spirit : No quantity.
8.  Intoxicating drugs : No quantity.

PENALTIES

Whoever in contravention of the Act, or of any Rule or order issued or
passed there under or license or permit granted or issued under this Act.

(a) Imports, exports, transports manufactures, collect or possess or sell
any intoxicant.

(b) Uses, keeps or has in his possession any materials, stills utensils,
implements or apparatus whatsoever for the purpose of manufacturing
any intoxicating other than toddy.

Came into force with effect on and from the 17th June, 2017 & 8th

All offences are non-bailable.
All offences are cognizable.

sub-section (1) of section 3, section 4,section 5, section 6, section 7 and
section 8 shall be deemed to have come into force with effect on and
from the 17th June, 2017.

sub-section (2) of section 2 and section 9 shall be deemed to have come
into force with effect on and from the 8th July, 2017

As per Sec 6. Where any instruments of gaming are found in any place
entered or searched, on or about the person found therein, it shall be
presumed that such place is used as a common gaming house and that
the persons found therein were present there for the purpose of gaming
although no gaming was actually seen by the police officer or any of his
assistants.

As per Sec 7. Nothing in the Explanation to section 4, or in section 6

whether incorporated or not, unless such persons are actually found
gaming or facilitating such gaming in any manner in such premises or
place.

a game of skill?

The Supreme Court has identified the specific components which makes
rummy a game of skill. In the case ofAndhra Pradesh v.
Satyanarayana, AIR 1968 SC 825, it has held that:

Rummy...requires certain amount of skill because the fall of the cards
has to be memorised and the building up of Rummy requires
considerable skill in holding and discarding cards. We cannot,
therefore, say that the game of Rummy is a game of entire chance. It is
mainly and preponderantly a game of skill.

However, while holding that Rummy is a game of skill, the Court has
placed some restrictions on organizing the game, and the judgment is in

f there is evidence of gambling in some other way or that the owner of

THE TELANGANA PREVENTION OF DISFIGUREMENT OF
OPEN PLACES AND PROHIBITION OF OBSCENE AND
OBJECTIONABLE POSTERS AND ADVERTISEMENT ACT,
1997

Section-2 Definitions:- In this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires,-

a)”Advertisement” Includes any notice, circular, hand-bill label, wrapper
or other document and also includes any visible representation made by
means of any light, sound, smoke or gas.

(b)”Authority” means an authority constituted under section-9
c)”Government” means the state Government of Andhra Pradesh.

d)”’Notification” means a notification published in the Andhra Pradesh
Gazette and the word “notified” shall be construed accordingly.

e)”Objectionable advertisement” means any advertisement.

i) Which is likely to incite any person to commit murder, sabotage or any
offence involving violence, or

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
i) Which is likely to incite any person to commit murder, sabotage or any
offence involving violence, or

ii) Which is likely to seduce any member of the armed forces of the Union or
of the Police forces from allegiance or his duty, or prejudice the recruiting
of persons to serve in any such force or prejudice the discipline of any
such force, or

iii) Which is likely to incite any section of the citizens of India to commit an
act of violence against any other section of the citizens of India or,

iv) Which is deliberately intended to outrage the religious feelings of any
class of the citizens of India by insulting or blaspheming or profaning the
religion or the religious beliefs of that else,

(v) Which is grossly incident, or scurrilous or obscene or is intended to

black-mail,

f)’Place open to public view” includes any private place or building,
monument, statue, post-wall, fence, tree or other thing or contrivance
visible to a person being in, or passing along, any public place,

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
g)’Poster” means any printed, typed, hand written, cyclostyled or Xeroxed
matter or design or pictorial representation usually meant to be
displayed as a play card or pasted on any wall, building, hoarding or
other place open to public view whether by cinematography exhibition or
otherwise but does not include the exhibition of a cinematography film
inside the auditorium of a cinema theatre.

240 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(i)”Public place” means any place (including a road, street or way, whether a
thoroughfare or not and a landing place) to which the public are granted
access of have a right to resort, or ever which they have a right to pass.

Sec.3. Penalty for disfigurement by objectionable advertisement:

Whoever affixes to, or inscribes or exhibits in any place open to public
view any objectionable advertisement, shall be punished with imprisonment
of either description for a term which may extend to one year or with fine
which shall not be less than two thousand rupees but which may extend to
five thousand rupees or with both.

Sec.4. Penalty for unauthorized disfigurement by advertisement:-

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Sec.4. Penalty for unauthorized disfigurement by advertisement:-

Whoever affixes to, or inscribes or exhibits on any place open to public
view any advertisement without the written consent of the owner or occupier
or person in management of the property in which such place is situated
shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which
may extend to three months or with fine which shall not be less than one
thousand rupees but which may extend to two thousand rupees, or with
both.

Sec. 5. Punishment of abettors : Whoever in any manner whatsoever
causes, procures, counsels, aids, abets or is accessory to, the Commission
of any offence under Section 3 or Section 4 shall be punished with the
punishment provided for the offence.

Sec. 6. Power to remove, erase, pull down and destroy objectionable
advertisements :

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Sec. 6. Power to remove, erase, pull down and destroy objectionable
advertisements :

(1) Any police officer not below the rank of Sub-Inspector or any other
officer empowered in this behalf by the Government may remove,
erase, or otherwise pull down any objectionable advertisement.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(2) Any advertisement removed or pulled down under sub-section (1) ora
photograph of any advertisement erased under that sub-section shall
be produced before a Magistrate of the First Class and if, in the opinion
of the Magistrate, such advertisement is an objectionable
advertisement, the Magistrate may cause the advertisement or the
photograph there of to be destroyed after giving an opportunity of
hearing to the advertiser whoever he is known and where he is not
known after recording that fact, but it in the opinion of the Magistrate
such advertisement is not an objectionable advertisement the
Magistrate shall dispose it of in the manner provided in Sections 457,
458 and 459 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 or 457 in the
case of an advertisement which is erased make an order that it shall
be restored at the cost of the Government.

241 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Sec. 7. Obscene Poster : A poster shall be deemed to be obscene if,-

a) its effect is to tend to debase and computer persons who are likely
having regard to all relevant circumstances, to read or see the after
container or embodied in it;

(b) it holds out or recommends to the public anything to be used as, or
suggestive of, a sexual stimulant.

c) it undermines the accepted cannons of decency or encourages vicious
or immoral acts.

d) it lowers the sacredness of the institution of marriage or depicts
scenes of rape, criminal assault on women or other immoralities,

e) it exhibits the human form ina state of nudity or indecorous or
sensual posture, or

f) it encourages lasciviousness or arouses impure and lecherous
thoughts.

Sec. 8. Prohibiting of printing, publishing or displaying obscene
posters:

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf

page_content:
THE TELANGANA PROHIBITION OF RAGGING ACT, 1997
1. Definitions.—

(a)“Educational Institution” means and includes a college, or other
institution by whatever name called, carrying on the activity or imparting
education therein (either exclusively or among other activities); and includes
an orphanage or boarding home or hostel or a tutorial institution or any
other premises attached thereto ;

(b)“Ragging” means doing an act which causes or is likely to cause
insult or annoyance of fear or apprehension or threat or intimidation or
outrage of modesty or injury to a student;

(c)‘Student” means a person who is admitted to an educational
institution and whose name is lawfully borne on the attendance register
thereof;

2. Prohibition of Ragging.— within or outside any educational institution .
3. Penalty for Ragging.—

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Whoever, with the intention of causing ragging or with the knowledge
that he is likely by such act to cause ragging, commits or abets ragging and
thereby,—(i)teases or embarrasses or humiliates a student shall be punished
with imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months or with fine
which may extend to one thousand rupees or with both; or(iijassaults or
uses criminal force to or criminally intimidates a student shall be punished
with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year or with fine
which may extend to two thousand rupees or with both; or(iii)wrongfully
restrains or wrongfully confines or causes hurt to a student shall be
punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years or
with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees or with both;
or(iv)causes grievous hurt to or kidnaps or abducts or rapes or commits
unnatural offence with a student shall be punished with imprisonment for a

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
or(iv)causes grievous hurt to or kidnaps or abducts or rapes or commits
unnatural offence with a student shall be punished with imprisonment for a
term which may extend to five years and with fine which may extend to ten
thousand rupees; or(v)causes death or abets suicide shall be punished with
imprisonment for life or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to
ten years and with a fine which may extend to fifty thousand rupees.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
4. Dismissal of student.—(1)A student convicted of an offence under section
4 and punished with imprisonment for a term shall be dismissed from the
educational institution.(2)A student convicted of an offence under section 4
and punished with imprisonment for a term of more than six months shall
not be admitted in any other educational institution.

5. Suspension of student.
6. Abetment.

page_content:
THE PREVENTION OF INSULTS TO NATIONAL HONOUR ACT, 1971
1. Insult to Indian National Flag and Constitution Of India

Whoever in any public place or in any other place within public view
burns, mutilates, defaces, defiles, disfigures, destroys, tramples upon or
*otherwise shows disrespect to or brings into contempt shall be punished
with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine,
or with both.

Explanation 1 - The expression, “Indian National Flag” includes any
picture, painting, drawing or photograph, or other visible representation of
the Indian National Flag, or of any part or parts thereof, made of any
substance or represented on any substance.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Explanation 2 - The disrespect to the Indian National flag means and
includes — a gross affront or indignity offered to the Indian National Flag; or
dipping the Indian National Flag in salute to any person or thing; or flying
the Indian National Flag at half-mast except on occasions on which the Flag
is flown at half-mast on public buildings in accordance with the instructions
issued by the Government; or using the Indian National Flag as a drapery in
any form whatsoever except in state funerals or armed forces or other para-
military forces funerals; or using the Indian National Flag:- (i) as a portion of
costume, uniform or accessory of any description which is worn below the
waist of any person; or (ii) by embroidering or printing it on cushions,
handkerchiefs, napkins, undergarments or any dress material; or putting
any kind of inscription upon the Indian National Flag; or using the Indian
National Flag as a receptacle for receiving, delivering or carrying anything

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
National Flag as a receptacle for receiving, delivering or carrying anything
except flower petals before the Indian National Flag is unfurled as part of
celebrations on special occasions including the Republic Day or the
Independence Day; or using the Indian National Flag as covering for a statue
or a monument or a speaker’s desk or a speaker’s platform; or allowing the
Indian National Flag to touch the ground or the floor or trail in water
intentionally; or draping the Indian National Flag over the hood, top, and
sides or back or on a vehicle, train, boat or an aircraft or any other similar
object; or using the Indian National Flag as a covering for a building; or
intentionally displaying the Indian National Flag with the “saffron” down.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
2. Prevention Of Singing Of National Anthem shall be punished with
imprisonment for a term, which may extend to three years, or with fine, or
with both.

2 a Minimum Penalty On Second Or Subsequent Offence shall be
punishable for the second and for every subsequent offence, with
imprisonment for a term, which shall not be less than one year.

THE TELANGANA PREVENTION OF ANTI-SOCIAL AND HAZARDOUS
ACTIVITIES ACT, 1980.

1. Definitions
(a)"Goonda" means a person who,—

(b)either by himself or as a member or leader of a gang, habitually commits
or attempts to commit or abets the commission of, offences punishable
under section 294, Chapter XVI, Chapter XVII, or Chapter XXII of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860; or

(iiJhaving once been convicted of an offence under one or more of the
following Acts is again convicted under—

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(a) The Opium Act, 1878 (b) The Dangerous Drugs Act, 1930.(c) The Drugs
and Cosmetics Act, 1940.(d) The Prevention of Food Adulteration Act,
1954.(e) The Essential Commodities Act, 1955.(f) The Suppression of
Immoral Traffic in Women and Girls Act, 1956 .(g) Sections 25, 26, 27,
28 or 29 of the Arms Act, 1959 .(h) The Customs Act, 1962 .(i) The
Foreign Exchange Regulation Act, 1973 .(j) The Andhra Pradesh Excise
Act, 1968 .(k) The Andhra Pradesh Gaming Act, 1974 .(l) The Andhra
Pradesh Prevention of Begging Act, 1977 . orf(iii) has been found
habitually passing indecent remarks to or teasing, women or girls;
or(iv)has been found habitual in intimidation of law abiding people by
acts of violence or by show of force; or(v)is habituated—(a)to commit
affray or breach of peace, riot; or(b)to make forcible collection or
subscription, or to threaten people for illegal pecuniary gain for himself
or for others; or(c)to cause alarm, danger, or harm to persons or
property;

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
2. Externment of Goondas

(1)Where it appears to the Commissioner or the District Magistrate(a)that
any person is a goonda; and(b)(i)that his movements or acts in the
district or any part thereof are causing or are calculated to cause alarm,
danger or harm to persons or property; or(ii)that there are reasonable
grounds for believing that he is engaged or about to engage in the district
or any part thereof, in the commission or abetment, of any offence or act;
(c)that witnesses are not willing to come forward to give evidence against
him by reason of apprehension on their part as regards the safety of their
person or property; the Commissioner or the District Magistrate shall, by
notice in writing, inform him of the general nature of the material
allegations against him in respect of clauses (a), (b) and (c) and give him
a reasonable opportunity of tendering an explanation regarding them.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
(2)The Commissioner or the District Magistrate on being satisfied that the
conditions specified, may by order in writing—(a)direct him to remove

249 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
himself outside the district or part thereof, as the case may be, by such
route, such time as may be specified in the order, until the expiry of such
period, not exceeding six months, as may be specified in the order;

3. Permission to return temporarily

The Commissioner or the District Magistrate may, by an order, permit
any person in respect of whom an order has been made to enter or return,
for a temporary period, into or to the area subject to such conditions and
may, at any time rescind any such permission.

4. Extension of period of order

In the interest of the general public, but the period so extended shall in
no case exceed two years in the aggregate.

5. Appeal

1. Any person aggrieved by an order made under section 3, section 4 or
section 5 may appeal to the Tribunal within fifteen days from the date of
service of the order in the manner prescribed by rules made.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
2. The Tribunal may confirm the order, with or without modification, or set
it aside, and may pending disposal of the appeal, stay the operation of
the order subject to such terms, if any, as it thinks fit.

6. Punishment for contravention of orders und sections 3 to 6:

Whoever contravenes any order made under section 3, section 4
section 5 or section 6 shall be punishable with rigorous imprisonment for a
term which may extend to three years but shall not be less than six months,
and with fine which may extend to three thousand rupees but shall not be
less than one thousand rupees.

7. Forcible removal of external Goonda reentering, etc., contravention
of order

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
7. Forcible removal of external Goonda reentering, etc., contravention
of order

1. Where, after an order is made against a person under section 3, section
4, section 5, or section 6, such person,— (a) has failed to remove himself
from the district or as directed by the order, or (b) has re-entered the
area, may cause him to be arrested and removed in police custody to
such place outside the area specified in the said order as he may direct.

2. Any officer-in charge of the police station may arrest without warrant any
person reasonably suspected of an act or omission specified in
subsection (1), to the nearest Executive Magistrate.

8. Cognizance of offence

No Magistrate shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under
section 10, except—(a) upon a report in writing of the facts constituting such

250 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
offence made by an officer-in charge of the police station; or (b) upon
information received from any person other than an officer-in charge of the
police station, or upon his own knowledge or suspicion, that such offence
has been committed.

page_content:
v. Eve Teasing

Social Vices - Drinking — Consumption of Alcohol not only leads to various
health hazards but, it is a root - cause for many social evils. An inebriated /
intoxicated person often looses his cool and temperament due to which all
cardial relationship in the society in general and the family in particular get
disintegrated, and paves way for despair and distress. Drunk an driving is a
major concern in the present scenario as there is rapid increase in death
rate due to accidents. Despite several special drives, strict enforcement,
counselling sessions, and suspension of driving licence, drunken driving
violations have not decreased and then is a still long way to go by sensitizing
the masses.

page_content:
TELANAGANA STATE POLICE DEPARTMENT

STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE
FOR
CONDUCT OF DRIVE AGAINST DRUNK DRIVING

1.0 PURPOSE

Police generally enforce drunken driving laws by a “Selective breath
Checkpoint” strategy.Identifying vulnerable locations and _ systematic
crackdowns throughout the year held in reduce traffic accidents or deaths due
to drunk driving.

2.0 POLICY

Telangana State Police has launched enforcement of ananti-drunk
driving program in all Police Stations across the State to avoid traffic deaths
and accidents. The drive facilitates reducing the accidents drastically.

3.0 ENFORCEMENT

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
3.0 ENFORCEMENT

Driving under the influence of alcohol is a punishable offence. The
drunken drivers shall be booked u/s 185 (a) Motor Vehicles Act, 1989 and
prosecuted in the Magistrate Court concerned. Under Section 185 (a) M.V
Act, whoever, while driving or attempting to drive a motor vehicle has, in
his blood, alcohol exceeding 30 mg. per 100 ml. of blood detected in a test by
a breath analyzer, shall be punishable for the first offence with
imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which
may extend to two thousand and more rupees, or with both; and for a
second or subsequent offence, if committed within three years of the
commission of the previous similar offence, with imprisonment for a term
which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to three
thousand rupees, or with both.

Note: Tolerance may be given upto 35 mg per 100 ml.
3.1 Location of the Checkpoints

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Note: Tolerance may be given upto 35 mg per 100 ml.
3.1 Location of the Checkpoints

To test the effectiveness of surprise checkpoints at random locations
compared to fixed checkpoints, Police Stations have to identify random
locations to hold their checkpoints and catch drunk drivers.

3.2 Frequency of Checkpoints

To identify variations in the duration of checkpoints, Police Stations
have to organize checkpoints randomly at different days in a week and at
different time periods. Frequency can be higher on Fridays / Saturdays.

Procedure to be adhered to at the spot

While checking the drivers at random, use passive mode of Breath
Analyser and if it confirms drunkenness of the driver take another test
with active mode for obtaining the test receipt.

In case of any representation on procedure of Breath Analyser, the
driver should be given an option for Lab test (Examination by a doctor)

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
In case of any representation on procedure of Breath Analyser, the
driver should be given an option for Lab test (Examination by a doctor)

Take photographs while taking the test, and ensure the respondent,
vehicle with number and test process are taken in 3 to 4 pictures. It
can be used as evidence in the case of contest.

Print two Breath Analyser test result slips and get them duly signed by
the respondent. Give one slip to the respondent and keep another slip
to attach it to the chargesheet as evidence.Keep one photo copy of the
slip for the police station record along with the charge sheet.

Issue E-ticket u/s 185 (a), 130/177 MV Act. Detain the vehicle and
send it to the Police Station in the interest of safety of the person and
citizen at large. Handover the copy of e-Ticket to the respondent, while
issuinge - ticket to the respondent take his photograph through TAB.

Detain the vehicle only, not the documents or respondent.

page_content:
Detain the vehicle only, not the documents or respondent.

Inform the respondent to come to the Police Station on the next day for
counseling and attending Court.

Personal safety of Police Staff from the speeding vehicles — Be careful
while stopping the vehicles. Wear Reflective Jackets. Position the
barricades in such a way that the speed of vehicles is reduced on
reaching the place of drive.

v Safety of the drivers. Stop them carefully and take them to the extreme
margin of the road.

v Escaping vehicles by taking ‘U’ turn. Don’t chase them to invite
another problem. Most of the escapists are those who do not carry
the documents and triple riders. Our aim is to prosecute drunken
drivers.

v Arguments, threats, recommendations - Politely tell them to keep cool
and inform them about further legal complications i.e., obstruction of
legal duty and misbehavior with the public servant u/s 353 IPC and
186 IPC. Even if they continue with their faulty behavior, inform to
your superior officers and take the sufficient force for assistance and
further legal proceedings. In the circumstances stated, please record
the incident in the video.

v In case drunken driver accompanied by friends or family members,
especially women and children — facilitate them with a taxi or auto.

Honoured Sir,

Brief facts of the case are that on (Date), at (Time), the LW-2 while
conducting “Enforcement against Drunken Driving” at Secunderabad
Railway Station check point, at random, stopped one Two wheeler motor
cycle/Car/Auto/Goods Vehicle bearing No: with the help of the LW- 1.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
The respondent who was the driver of Vehicle, Sri.
S/o, age. years,
Occupation: R/O
was subjected to Alcohol Test with Breath Analyzer by LW - 2. The
printed result of the test shown the BAC level as __ mg/100ml that has
exceeded the permissible BAC level of 30 mg/ 100 ml as mentioned in
section 185 (a) MV Act. Photos were taken while taking the breath test.
Lw-2 obtained the signature of the respondent on the reading slip as well
as on the MV Act Challan Receipt. Under the above circumstances, the
BAC levels established that the respondent consumed alcohol more than
the permissible limits. As such, allowing the respondent to drive the
vehicle was not advisable in view of public safety as well as the
respondent himself. Thus, in good faith and as the respondent was not
carrying the documents, LW 2 detained the vehicle. The respondent was
issued with the original Breath Analyzer test result and the original MV
Act Challan Receipt to appear before the Hon’ble Court.

page_content:

Sheet is liable for punishment u/s 185 (a), 130/177 M.V.Act.

Gambling: Gambling has been prevalent in our society since ancient times.
It is also considered as one of the social evils endangering the growth and
development of our society. Many stringent measures in the form of laws and
acts have been enacted so as to curb this menace. The Telangana Gambling
Act — Amendment Act 2017 includes on line gaming and skill game is also an
offence apart from game of chance and regular gambling.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
PROSTITUTION/ SEX WORKERS: Prostitution is also considered as a
social evil, it is one of the oldest professions in the world. Prostitution is
considered taboo in India and is not discussed openly and a topic
frequently frowned upon. The abolition of prostitution is not plausible
as it is an ancient practice and has existed too long. However, by
imposing Prevention of Immoral Traffic Act, this menace is being
controlled but has not been totally eradicated.

Illegal bars

The Illegal Bars or Pubs are run by the owners of Bars/ Pubs without
proper licenses/ unlicensed Bars or Pubs which do not adhere to the rules
and regulations of the Govt. The Telangana High Court said that no music
can be played in Hyderabad pubs after 10 pm. The court further raised
questions as to how pubs were allowed to operate in residential areas and
near school zones, since this is not allowed under the Excise Act. All the
violations of illegal bars/ pubs are dealt under the Telangana Excise Act.

Rowdies

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Rowdies

260 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
The history sheeted persons/ the bad characters - who have been
convicted for bodily offences and other unsocial elements indulging in
disturbing the peace and tranquility of the locality such as notorious rowdy
sheeters residing staying in the locality are kept under constant monitoring/
surveillance in order to maintain peace.

They are committing offences including brutal murder using lethal
weapons along with their associates in an organized manner, adversely
affecting public order and their Acts cause widespread fear, terror and panic
among the general public, thereby disturbing the peace, tranquility and
social harmony in the society. As part of an ongoing drive against notorious
habitual offenders who disturb public order, a number of rowdy sheeters,
goondas and criminals are being detained to reduce crime and maintain
peace.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
The Rowdy activities are controlled by the Telangana Prevention of
Dangerous Activities of Boot-leggers, Dacoits, Drug-Offenders, Goondas,
Immoral Traffic Offenders Land-Grabbers, Spurious Seed Offenders,
Insecticide Offenders, Fertiliser Offenders, Food Adulteration Offenders, Fake
Document Offenders, Scheduled Commodities Offenders, Forest Offenders,
Gaming Offenders, Sexual Offenders, Explosive Substances Offenders, Arms
Offenders, Cyber Crime Offenders and White Collar or Financial Offenders
Act, 1986.

Encroachments/ Wrong Parkings

Unauthorised encroachments are a major concern as it disrupts the free
flow of traffic and leads to traffic jams/ congestions. Due to the
encroachments of pavements pedestrains too face lot of problems. In order to
remove encroachments on road, police should conduct awareness meetings
with local leaders/ municipal/ revenue officials and sensitize the general
public, issue notices to the un-authorised encroachers and lastly remove the
encroachment.

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Due to wrong parkings/ haphazard parkings traffic jams occurs quite
frequently resulting in considerably movement of vehicles. The hapazard/
wrong parkings are mostly seen on the main carriage way/ roads infront of
Hotels, Bars & Restaurants, food vendors, small retail vendors, fruit
vendors, cycle repairs shops, two wheelers mechanic shops, vegetable
vendors and flower vendors, fish markets etc. and the encroachments are
quite visible abutting the roads i.e. mostly on the footpaths. The
encroachments are removed by traffic police and cases are booked against
the violators under 39 (b), Hyderabad City Police Act and for wrong parking
under relevant provisions of MV Act. The Hyderabad Traffic Police conducts
special drive to de-congest the roads through ‘ROPE’ - ‘Removal of

261 | Page

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Obstructive Parking and Encroachments’, compulsory implementation of
‘Stop Line’ and ‘Free Left’ ensures smooth and free flow of traffic.

Late closing of shops and restaurants

All the shops and Business Establishments are supposed to be closed
on scheduled time. However, the shops and restaurants having obtained
permissions from concerned Commissionerates are allowed as per the
conditions laid down in the permission. The violators of late closing of shops
and business establishments are booked under Hyderabad City Police Act.

Begging

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Begging

Begging is a socio-economic issue of great concern in our country. It is
associated with the problems of poverty and unemployment. Begging is a
problem for society in as much as a large number of beggars means non
utilization of available human resources, and a drag upon the existing
resources of the society. It is common to find beggars at rubbish dumps,
road sides, and traffic lights and under flyovers. The frail, crippled and
mentally ill share space with children, women and able bodied men on the
roads. Comprehensive measures are being taken by the Govt. for the
rehabilitation of the beggars and stringent action are also initiated against
them in order to curb the begging problem by registering cases under
Telangana Prevention of Begging Act, 1977.

Eve Teasing

metadata.filename: Maintenance of Public Peace and Order (02.04.2024).pdf
metadata.subject: public_order_ethics

page_content:
Eve Teasing

Eve teasing is an act of harassing women playfully or maliciously,
physically or psychologically to make fun, irritate, provoke, annoy,
embarrass through comments, remarks, gestures, jokes, physical contact
and taunts.

In order to provide safety and to ensure security of women, SHE Teams,
Bharosa Centers & women safety wings functions 24x7. The SHE Teams
Staff move in mufti at Hot Spots — like bus stops, colleges, and other
educational institutions to nab men who harass women. Often, the
offenders are caught passing lewd comments, stalking women and making
indecent gestures, but some even go to the extent of physical assault. Before
nabbing the culprits, the SHE Teams make a brief and quick video
recording of the abuse to use as evidence. Counseling for offenders’
alongwith their parents are conducted by the SHE Teams and Bharosa
Centres and cases are also booked under relevant provisions of law.
